raw_response:
Based on the Q4 2025 evidence, Helix has a defined incident response program and there is strong evidence of effective execution for some incidents, particularly INC-2025-804. However, several gaps were identified in post-mortem quality, reviewer approval, corrective action governance, and communication evidence.

| # | Finding Title | Document / Section | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SEV-2 post-mortem for INC-2025-803 does not meet policy quality standards and remains unapproved | Incident Response Policy §4.2, §4.3; Post-mortem INC-2025-803; Incident Register | The INC-2025-803 post-mortem is only a short summary and was explicitly returned for revision by the Security Operations Manager. It lacks required elements: detailed UTC timeline, 5-Whys root cause analysis, quantified impact assessment, remediation details, detection improvement recommendations, named owners/Jira references, and reviewer sign-off. The register still shows the post-mortem as “Complete,” but reviewer sign-off is “No — returned for revision,” indicating the required review cycle was not completed. | High |
| 2 | Incident register status does not accurately reflect post-mortem approval state | Incident Register; Incident Response Policy §4.3 | INC-2025-803 is marked “Complete” even though the reviewer returned it for revision and there is no evidence of final approval. This creates ambiguity over whether management considers the post-mortem complete before reviewer sign-off. Under the policy, post-mortems should be signed off or returned with revisions; a returned item should not be treated as complete until corrected and approved. | Medium |
| 3 | INC-2025-806 post-mortem was approved despite not meeting the minimum preventive action item requirement | Incident Response Policy §4.2.5; Post-mortem INC-2025-806 | The policy requires a minimum of three preventive action items for each SEV-1 and SEV-2 post-mortem. INC-2025-806 includes only two action items. Although the report is otherwise adequate and was approved by the Security Operations Manager, approval of a post-mortem that does not meet a stated quality standard weakens consistency of the review process. | Medium |
| 4 | Detection improvement section for INC-2025-806 is weak and does not fully address lessons learned | Incident Response Policy §4.2.6; Post-mortem INC-2025-806 | The policy requires detection improvement recommendations. INC-2025-806 states “Recommendation: None — existing detection and auto-mitigation were effective.” While Cloudflare response was effective, the incident still revealed endpoint-level rate limiting gaps. The post-mortem could have considered more specific detection improvements, such as per-endpoint volumetric anomaly alerting, sync-endpoint traffic baselines, or earlier customer-impact alerting. | Low |
| 5 | Preventive action items do not consistently include named individual owners and Jira references | Incident Response Policy §4.2.5, §4.4; Post-mortems INC-2025-803, INC-2025-804, INC-2025-806 | The policy requires each preventive action item to include a named individual owner, priority, target completion date, and Jira ticket reference. INC-2025-803 action items list broad owners such as “Engineering” and “Security Ops” and do not include Jira references. INC-2025-804 includes several action items assigned to teams only, such as “DevOps Team,” “Security Ops,” and “Network Team,” rather than named individuals. This can weaken accountability and follow-through. | Medium |
| 6 | Corrective action governance evidence is incomplete and shows overdue items | Incident Response Policy §4.4; Q4 Security Operations Metrics Dashboard; Incident Register | The policy requires post-mortem action items to be entered in Jira, reviewed weekly, and escalated when overdue. The Q4 dashboard shows 30 action items created, 23 completed within the quarter, and 3 overdue. The provided evidence does not show weekly Monday Security Standup review records, Jira aging reports, or escalation evidence to the CISO/CTO for overdue items. This limits the ability to verify that lessons learned are consistently driving timely corrective action. | Medium |
| 7 | Detailed post-mortem evidence was not provided for all required Q4 SEV-1/SEV-2 incidents | Incident Response Policy §4.1, §4.2; Incident Register; Q4 Metrics Dashboard | The incident register and metrics indicate six Q4 incidents required post-mortems: two SEV-1 and four SEV-2 incidents. Detailed post-mortems were provided for only three: INC-2025-803, INC-2025-804, and INC-2025-806. Detailed reports for INC-2025-801, INC-2025-802, and INC-2025-805 were not provided. As a result, post-mortem completeness, quality, reviewer sign-off, customer/regulatory communication, and action item tracking cannot be independently validated for the full required population. | Medium |
| 8 | Communication evidence is absent for incidents where notification may have been required | Incident Response Policy §5.1, §5.2; Incident Register | The policy requires customer notification for data breaches within 48 hours and GDPR supervisory authority notification within 72 hours where applicable. INC-2025-801 involved unauthorized access to enterprise document storage and internal S3 buckets containing customer documents, which appears likely to require customer and possibly regulatory notification. No customer notification records, regulatory assessment, DPO determination, or contractual notification evidence were provided. This is a key CC7.3 element because the control requires communication of incidents as appropriate. | High |
| 9 | SEV-1 resolution SLA exception for INC-2025-804 was not explained | Incident Response Policy §3; Incident Register; Post-mortem INC-2025-804 | The policy states SEV-1 incidents should be resolved within 24 hours. INC-2025-804 had a resolution time of 35.8 hours. The post-mortem provides a clear timeline and strong remediation detail, and containment occurred within SLA, but it does not explicitly explain the resolution SLA miss or document management acceptance of the exception. | Low |

Overall, Helix demonstrates a functioning incident response process, but readiness for CC7.3 would be stronger if all required post-mortems were complete, reviewer-approved, consistently evidence-backed, and tied to accountable corrective action tracking and communication records.
